  "internal_reasoning": "Visually, this work is a large-scale abstract color-field painting consisting of two stacked, soft-edged rectangular zones: an upper warm brownish-ochre field and a lower deep crimson-red field, separated and surrounded by a luminous yellow ground. The composition, scale, feathered transitions, and stained/scrubbed handling strongly evoke the visual language of postwar American Color Field painting, and more specifically the idiom associated with Mark Rothko and painters working in his orbit. That said, because I have been given no title, signature, date, dimensions, reverse, provenance, exhibition history, or technical data, I cannot responsibly assign it to any specific artist. Therefore the valuation must be based only on what is visible and on the market for an unattributed but aesthetically convincing work in this manner.\n\nArtistically, the picture has considerable power. The chromatic structure is simple but effective: the hot yellow perimeter energizes the entire canvas and produces a glow around the two hovering fields. The upper form is thinner, dustier, and more atmospheric; the lower form is denser and more saturated, creating a weighted vertical tension. The soft, frayed edges suggest hand-worked brush application and wiping rather than mechanical masking. The painting appears to seek meditative emotional resonance rather than geometric precision, which aligns with serious mid-century abstraction. The balance is strong and the color relationships are sophisticated enough that the work reads as more than decorative. It has genuine visual conviction.\n\nHowever, fair market value at a major international auction depends overwhelmingly on authorship. If this were by a canonical figure such as Rothko, Still, Newman, or a first-rank follower, the value could range from high six figures into tens of millions depending on date, size, and provenance. But absent identification, the market discounts radically. Auction houses do not generally ascribe major values to works that merely resemble important schools. An “attributed,” “circle of,” “manner of,” or “after” designation would change the level somewhat, but with no visible signature and no metadata, the most prudent category is an anonymous or unattributed abstract painting in the style of mid-20th-century Color Field art.\n\nCondition, from the image alone, appears broadly stable. I do not see obvious tears, major paint loss, severe abrasions, or structural deformations. The canvas looks relatively flat and evenly stretched. That said, there may be surface grime, scattered accretions, subtle rubs, or restorations that cannot be detected from the front image. The intentionally abraded, vaporous edges make condition reading difficult because wear and original handling can resemble one another. Still, nothing visible suggests catastrophic impairment.\n\nAs for comparables, unattributed or lesser-known abstract color-field works with strong decorative appeal and convincing period appearance can trade from a few thousand dollars to perhaps $20,000-$40,000 when dimensions are substantial and quality is high. Works by documented but secondary postwar abstractionists can bring more, often $30,000-$150,000, but that presumes a named artist with an established auction record. Since we have no such support here, I would place this in the upper decorative / lower scholarly market band: attractive enough to interest collectors, designers, and buyers looking for a Rothko-like presence without blue-chip pricing, but lacking the validation needed for a major-estate or museum-level result.\n\nIf offered today at a major international auction as an unidentified abstract painting, assuming authentic age and legally saleable status, I believe it would likely sell in the low five figures rather than the six-figure realm. A realistic fair market value would center around $18,000, with plausible variance from roughly $10,000 to $35,000 depending on size, medium confirmation, and how convincingly it appears to be period rather than later homage. My confidence is moderate-low because authorship uncertainty is the dominant determinant and cannot be resolved from the image alone.",

parsed.internal_reasoning:
Visually, this work is a large-scale abstract color-field painting consisting of two stacked, soft-edged rectangular zones: an upper warm brownish-ochre field and a lower deep crimson-red field, separated and surrounded by a luminous yellow ground. The composition, scale, feathered transitions, and stained/scrubbed handling strongly evoke the visual language of postwar American Color Field painting, and more specifically the idiom associated with Mark Rothko and painters working in his orbit. That said, because I have been given no title, signature, date, dimensions, reverse, provenance, exhibition history, or technical data, I cannot responsibly assign it to any specific artist. Therefore the valuation must be based only on what is visible and on the market for an unattributed but aesthetically convincing work in this manner.

Artistically, the picture has considerable power. The chromatic structure is simple but effective: the hot yellow perimeter energizes the entire canvas and produces a glow around the two hovering fields. The upper form is thinner, dustier, and more atmospheric; the lower form is denser and more saturated, creating a weighted vertical tension. The soft, frayed edges suggest hand-worked brush application and wiping rather than mechanical masking. The painting appears to seek meditative emotional resonance rather than geometric precision, which aligns with serious mid-century abstraction. The balance is strong and the color relationships are sophisticated enough that the work reads as more than decorative. It has genuine visual conviction.

However, fair market value at a major international auction depends overwhelmingly on authorship. If this were by a canonical figure such as Rothko, Still, Newman, or a first-rank follower, the value could range from high six figures into tens of millions depending on date, size, and provenance. But absent identification, the market discounts radically. Auction houses do not generally ascribe major values to works that merely resemble important schools. An “attributed,” “circle of,” “manner of,” or “after” designation would change the level somewhat, but with no visible signature and no metadata, the most prudent category is an anonymous or unattributed abstract painting in the style of mid-20th-century Color Field art.

Condition, from the image alone, appears broadly stable. I do not see obvious tears, major paint loss, severe abrasions, or structural deformations. The canvas looks relatively flat and evenly stretched. That said, there may be surface grime, scattered accretions, subtle rubs, or restorations that cannot be detected from the front image. The intentionally abraded, vaporous edges make condition reading difficult because wear and original handling can resemble one another. Still, nothing visible suggests catastrophic impairment.

As for comparables, unattributed or lesser-known abstract color-field works with strong decorative appeal and convincing period appearance can trade from a few thousand dollars to perhaps $20,000-$40,000 when dimensions are substantial and quality is high. Works by documented but secondary postwar abstractionists can bring more, often $30,000-$150,000, but that presumes a named artist with an established auction record. Since we have no such support here, I would place this in the upper decorative / lower scholarly market band: attractive enough to interest collectors, designers, and buyers looking for a Rothko-like presence without blue-chip pricing, but lacking the validation needed for a major-estate or museum-level result.

If offered today at a major international auction as an unidentified abstract painting, assuming authentic age and legally saleable status, I believe it would likely sell in the low five figures rather than the six-figure realm. A realistic fair market value would center around $18,000, with plausible variance from roughly $10,000 to $35,000 depending on size, medium confirmation, and how convincingly it appears to be period rather than later homage. My confidence is moderate-low because authorship uncertainty is the dominant determinant and cannot be resolved from the image alone.
key_factors: Strong visual resemblance to postwar Color Field abstraction
High aesthetic quality and effective chromatic balance
No visible signature, provenance, or artist identification
Apparently stable overall condition from the image
Auction value severely limited by anonymity despite decorative and artistic appeal
